coredata.dc:title: Amine–carbon quantum dots (CQDs–NH2) tailored polymeric loose nanofiltration membrane for precise molecular separation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Institution of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Institution of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Dan-Dan
Wang, Long
Yan, Xiang-Yu
Cao, Xue-Li
Shi, Ting
Sun, Shi-Peng
coredata.dc:description: Nanofiltration membranes show great potential in water treatment and resource recovery for their special separation mechanisms that can separate the small molecules effectively. However, their wide application needs to address the low separation efficiency caused by the similar solute sizes and charges while using the charged NF membranes with nanopores. This work reports a low-cost amine–carbon quantum dots (CQDs–NH2) modified thin-film composite (CTFC) membrane via the facile interfacial polymerization process. The electrostatic force and competing reaction between the positively charged CQDs–NH2 and the reactive monomers reformulated the interfacial polymerization process. The resultant CTFC membrane exhibits improved surface morphology and hydrophilicity. Besides, the incorporation of CQDs–NH2 changed the original polyamide network structures to construct more transport space. The CTFC membrane permeance was boosted significantly (from 6.2 to 38.44 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) compared with the unmodified TFC membrane, showing precise dyes separation property. This work may offer valuable guidance for the low-cost loose nanofiltration membrane preparation towards molecular separation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amine–carbon quantum dots
Interfacial polymerization
Nanofiltration membranes
Precise molecular separation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the CQDs–NH2 regulated polyamide nanofiltration membranes
Fabrication of the CQDs–NH2 regulated CTFC membranes
Effects of the CQDs–NH2 on the CTFC membrane structure and surface properties
The nanofiltration performance and possible modification mechanisms of the CQDs–NH2 modified CTFC membranes
Precise separation performance evaluation of the CTFC membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM analysis and (b) diameter distribution obtained by statistical analysis. (c) The ATR-FTIR analysis of the synthesized CQDs–NH2.

The SEM analysis of the prepared CQDs–TFC nanofiltration membranes with different CQDs–NH2 amounts modification (a: TFC; b: C0.5%TFC; c: C1%TFC; d: C1.5%TFC; e: C2%TFC). (f) The effect of CQDs–NH2 amounts on the membrane performance (1000 ppm Na2SO4 solution, 6 bar).
The surface chemical structure analysis of the membranes with different CQDs–NH2 concentrations.
(a)The water contact angle changes and (b) the XRD analysis of the membranes prepared with different CQDs–NH2 amounts.
(a) The surface element analysis of all membranes and the corresponding N1s deconvolution analysis of different membranes ((b) TFC; (c) C0.5%TFC; (d) C1%TFC; (e) C1.5%TFC; (d) C2%TFC).
(a) Zeta potentials and (b) corresponding isoelectric points of the membranes with different CQDs–NH2 addition.
The salt solution (1000 ppm) separation performance of the membranes prepared with different CQDs–NH2 amounts. (a) C0.5%TFC; (b) C1%TFC; (c) C1.5%TFC; (d) C2%TFC.
The membrane separation performances to dyes with different properties.
The possible schematics of membrane structures and performances formation in different interfacial polymerization (a–c: TFC; d–f: C2%TFC).
(a) Mixed dyes separation effect and corresponding UV–vis spectrum before and after separation. (b) The molecular structure of the two dyes.
The performance comparisons to the relevant researches about polyamide membranes.
The analysis of the N-containing groups on the membrane surfaces.

head.ce:title: Amine–carbon quantum dots (CQDs–NH2) tailored polymeric loose nanofiltration membrane for precise molecular separation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University Nanjing 211816, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Institute of Industrial Biotechnology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Tianjin, 300308, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Institute of Industrial Biotechnology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Tianjin, 300308, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofiltration membranes show great potential in water treatment and resource recovery for their special separation mechanisms that can separate the small molecules effectively. However, their wide application needs to address the low separation efficiency caused by the similar solute sizes and charges while using the charged NF membranes with nanopores. This work reports a low-cost amine–carbon quantum dots (CQDs–NH2) modified thin-film composite (CTFC) membrane via the facile interfacial polymerization process. The electrostatic force and competing reaction between the positively charged CQDs–NH2 and the reactive monomers reformulated the interfacial polymerization process. The resultant CTFC membrane exhibits improved surface morphology and hydrophilicity. Besides, the incorporation of CQDs–NH2 changed the original polyamide network structures to construct more transport space. The CTFC membrane permeance was boosted significantly (from 6.2 to 38.44 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) compared with the unmodified TFC membrane, showing precise dyes separation property. This work may offer valuable guidance for the low-cost loose nanofiltration membrane preparation towards molecular separation.

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane separation technologies including ultrafiltration (UF), reverse osmosis (RO), and nanofiltration (NF) are coming into focus due to their special physical, green, and energy-saving separation mechanisms (Cao et al., 2020; Ding et al., 2020a; Shang et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020). Among these technologies, the pore size distribution of nanofiltration membranes (0.5−2 nm) is in between RO and UF. Together with the Donnan effect caused by its charged surface, the NF membrane can realize the molecular scale separation (Liu et al., 2019; Paul and Jons, 2016; Yang et al., 2019b). Hence, NF membrane shows great potential in water treatment and resource recovery, especially in the high value-added industries, such as food, pharmaceutical, dyes, extraction of lithium, etc (Li et al., 2019, 2021; Razmjou et al., 2019).

The commercial NF membranes are mainly prepared via interfacial polymerization (IP) to form the TFC membranes (Yang et al., 2019a; Zhang et al., 2020). The IP reaction happens in a few seconds, forming a dense selective layer with high rejection to ions. Due to the rapid reaction rate, it is difficult to achieve the ideal permeability and selectivity through the traditional IP reaction (Yong and Zhang, 2021). Currently, most works concentrate on designing new reactive monomers (Yuan et al., 2018, 2020), introducing the additives or nanoparticles (Cai et al., 2020; Cao et al., 2019), self-assembly, or metal coordination to control the membrane structures (Meng et al., 2020; Shan et al., 2018). Some works have proposed that the aqueous additives, such as amine co-monomer, hydrophilicity materials and co-solvent, may customize the membrane selectivity by controlling the the monomer diffusion and reformulating the IP process to form ideal TFC structures (Xue et al., 2020; Zhe et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2020). Jason et al. proved that new reactive monomers design may customize the selective layer structure with specific polyamide pores to realize the molecular separation (Yuan et al., 2020). Many works also focus on introducing the nanomaterials in two reaction solutions to influence the IP process to construct transport channels or form the loose polyamide structures for molecular sieving (Guo et al., 2021; Ji et al., 2019; Porter et al., 2020). Notably, the large amounts of chemical reagents exhausted in synthesizing the monomers or nanomaterials may increase the cost, bring resource waste, and burden the environment. Hence, developing environment-friendly additives or nanomaterials with low-cost is of no delay.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a kind of organic nanomaterials with great water solubility, rich functionalized groups, and excellent biocompatibility (Lim et al., 2015). Owing to the ultra-small particle size of about 3−10 nm, the CQDs show well disperse property in water, which will not make defects to the thin selective layer with nanoscale thickness. Besides, the CQDs are easy to be synthesized via the facile hydrothermal method, possessing a rich choice of low-cost raw materials. Therefore, the CQDs have been widely studied in preparing TFC separation membranes (Zhao and Chung, 2018). Chung et al. designed a novel thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane by incorporating the Na–CQDs into the aqueous solution (Hea et al., 2018). The membrane permeance increased from 5.4 to 10.4 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with high rejection to the heavy-metal ions. Wu et al. synthesized several CQDs materials added them to the aqueous solution to form the TFN layer. They proved that the CQDs with different functional groups would influence membrane performance (Sun and Wu, 2018). Among these studies, most works concentrate on enhancing the dense membrane permeance with minimum loss of rejections to the small-sized salts. However, the CQDs-based loose nanofiltration membranes with precise molecular separation property were seldomly studied. Besides, most works ascribe the membrane performance enhancement factors to the improved membrane surface hydrophilicity and charge property, the larger or smooth changes of membrane morphology, and free volume (Ding et al., 2020a; Meng et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2017a). The analysis of the effect of CQDs incorporation on the membrane chemical structures is still insufficient.

This work reports a CQDs–NH2 modified TFC membrane for precise molecule separation. (1) The well-dispersed positively charged CQDs–NH2 solution with 3−6 nm particle size was synthesized by taking the glucose, PEI, and PEG-400 with low cost as the precursor via a facile one-step hydrothermal method. (2) By incorporating the CQDs–NH2 into the aqueous solution, the CQDs–NH2 modified nanofiltration membrane permeance was boosted from 6.2 to 38.44 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. (3) The electrostatic force and competing reaction between the positively charged CQDs–NH2 and the reactive monomers reformulated the interfacial polymerization process, improving the surficial and structural characters to realize precise dye molecule separation. This work may offer valuable guidance to the customization of low-cost high-performance loose nanofiltration membranes towards the dyes separation and other industries with precise molecular separation needs.

Polyethersulfone (PES) UF membranes were prepared according to our previous study (Wang et al., 2019b). Taking the d-(+)-glucose (AR, Merck) and polyethyleneimine (PEI-600, ≥94%, Aladdin) as the precursor, polyethylene glycol (PEG-400, ≥average Mn 400, Aladdin) as the surfactant, the well-dispersed CQDs–NH2 solution was obtained. Piperazine (PIP, AR) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98%) supplied by Aladdin were used as monomers to form the polyamide selective layer. Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, 99.5%), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, ≥CP), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, ≥99.95%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%), rhodamine B (RhB, ≥94%), fast green FCF (FCF, ≥94%), reactive black 5 (RB5, ≥50%), rose bengal (RB, BR) and alcian blue (AB, ≥50%) purchased from Macklin were used to evaluate the membrane selective performance. The dye properties are listed in Table S1.

The procedure to synthesize the CQDs–NH2 solution was modified from our previous study (Shao et al., 2020). First, glucose, PEG-400, PEI-600 were dissolved in the DI water and stirred for 2 h to form homogeneous mixture. Afterward, the mixture was transferred into the Teflon vessel and reacted at 180 °C for 7 h to get the CQDs–NH2 solution. The product was used directly without any purification.

The synthesized CQDs–NH2 chemical components were tested by attenuated total reflection-fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Nicolet 8700, USA). A transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 2100, Japan) was used to observe the CQDs–NH2 particles. The CQDs–NH2 was first dispersed in DI water and ultrasonicated for 1 h before testing, avoiding the aggregation when the drop was dripped on a super-thin carbon film. The CQDs–NH2 particle diameter distribution was analyzed by the statistic method from the TEM images with more than 100 random dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the CQDs–NH2 regulated polyamide nanofiltration membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The unmodified membranes and CQDs–NH2 modified membranes were prepared by the IP method. The PES substrate was immersed into PIP water solution (2 wt%) for 2 min. Then the membrane surface was wiped, followed by immersing into the TMC hexane solution (0.01 wt%) for 1 min to obtain the unmodified membranes, named as TFC. The CQDs–NH2 modified membrane was prepared by changing the aqueous solution to a mixture of PIP and CQDs–NH2 (2 wt% PIP + n% CQDs–NH2), named as CnTFC membrane.

The membrane surface morphologies were tested by a scanning electronic microscope (SEM, Hitachi S4800). The freeze-dried membranes were spray-coated with gold nanoparticles for 20 s by a sputter coater to improve the charging phenomenon when testing. The SEM images were captured with 3 kV accelerating voltage and 5 μA working current.

A water contact angle instrument (DropMeter A100P) was used to test the membrane surface water contact angles (WCA) to evaluate the membrane hydrophilicity. Each sample was tested more than three times to ensure the results convincing.

The membrane internal free volume changes were tested by X-ray diffraction instrument (XRD, Miniflex600, Rigaku, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1540562 nm). The 2θ scanning ranges from 10° to 40° with a step of 0.02°.

An X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab250Xi, ThermoFisher, USA) was used to test the membrane elements and reveal the change of group types. The test was carried out with a CAE scanning mode using Mono Al Kα X-ray source (hν = 1486.6 eV) with a narrow spectrum step of 0.1 eV with an energy of 15 kV and intensity of 10 mA. The scanning area of the membrane is 20 μm × 20 μm.

A streaming potential instrument (SurPASS™ 3, Anton Paar, Austria) equipped with an electrokinetic analyzer was used to test the membrane electrical properties. HCl and NaOH solutions with equal concentration (0.1 mol L−1) were used to adjust the pH value ranging from 3.0 to 10.0. KCl solution (0.001 mol L−1) was employed to ascertain the zeta potential.

The NF membrane permeance and separation performance were evaluated in a lab-made filtration setup. All membranes were pre-tested for more than 30 min at 6 bar to ensure the membrane was operated in a steady-state (Long et al., 2018). The membrane pure water permeance (PWP) was calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
PWP =
Q
Δ P × A × t
where ΔP and A represent the applied pressure (bar) and membrane effective area (m2), Q is the collected permeate volume (L) within a certain time t (h). Different salts or dyes solutions were used to test the membrane separation performances, which can be evaluated by the solute rejection (R):
(2)
R =
1 −
C p
C f
× 100 %
where C p and C f represent the permeate and concentration. The salts and dyes concentrations were tested by the conductivity meter (FE30, Mettler-Toledo, Switzerland) and UV–vis spectrophotometer (A560, AOE) separately.

According to our previous work (Yang et al., 2020), a mixture of PEI-600, glucose, and PEG-400 was used to synthesize the CQDs–NH2 solution via a facile hydrothermal method. The TEM images proved that the well-dispersed 3−6 nm CQDs–NH2 was synthesized successfully (Fig. 1 a,b). The acromion at 2920 cm−1 and 2875 cm−1 in the ATR-FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1c) ascribes to –NH2–. The peak shown at 1563 cm−1 is –NH– deformation vibration. These peaks strongly prove that the PEI-400 molecules or segments have appeared on CQDs–NH2 surface or embedded in the structures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the CQDs–NH2 regulated CTFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The traditional TFC membrane prepare by interfacial polymerization showed a typical nodule structure (Fig. 2 a). It showed low water permeance (6.2 L m−2 h-1 bar-1) and high rejection (>98%) to the 1000 ppm Na2SO4 solution (Fig. 2f). After the CQDs–NH2 was introduced, the modified CTFC membrane performance increased with the CQDs–NH2 amounts, and the morphologies of the modified membranes were changed. The traditional nodule structure becomes larger until it disappears on the membrane top surface. The membrane surface was smoother (Fig. 2b–e). With the CQDs–NH2 amounts was changed from 0% to 2%, the membrane performance was increased from 6.2 to 38.44 L m−2 h−1 bar-1. The membrane rejections to the salts were decreased (Fig. 2f). The ATR-FTIR was used to test the membrane chemical structure, as shown in Fig. 3 . All TFC membranes show a specific peak of the amide group at 1638 cm-1, proving the successful formation of the polyamide selective layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the CQDs–NH2 on the CTFC membrane structure and surface properties
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of CQDs–NH2 increased the membrane permeance and changed the membrane morphology. Hence, the WCA was used to test the membrane hydrophilicity (Fig. 4 a). The WCA decreased with the CQDs–NH2 amounts increasing, showing that the membrane surface became more hydrophilic than the pristine TFC membrane. It means that water can spread faster on the modified CTFC membrane surface. Besides the membrane hydrophilicity, the internal structures of the selective layer may also influence water transport. Hence, the XRD was used to observe the membrane internal structure changes (Fig. 4b). The polymer was considered as a component consisting of amorphous and semicrystalline structures (Dutta et al., 2003). The water mainly transports in the membrane amorphous networks. The stronger peaks mean the membrane has a more crystal structure, where water cannot transport (Jia et al., 2014). We can easily conclude that the addition of CQDs–NH2 makes the polyamide network structures disorder and the membranes showed more amorphous structures to permeate the water molecules. Controlling by the hydrophilic surface and amorphous structures with more transport paths, the membrane permeance was increased significantly than that of the traditional TFC membrane (from 6.2 to 38.44 L m−2 h−1 bar−1).

Based on the morphology, hydrophilicity, and internal structural changes of the CQDs–NH2 modified CTFC membranes, the XPS was used to observe the membrane surface chemical element components variations. All the membrane surfaces showed the N1s peak after the IP process, proving the successful formation of the polyamide selective layer (Fig. 5 a). The obtained details were shown in Table S2. When the CQDs–NH2 concentration was increased from 0.5% to 2%, the amounts of N1s increased from 12.17% to 12.24% and then it decreased to 11.04%, proving that the addition of CQDs–NH2 changed the interfacial polymerization reaction. Hence, the N1s were deconvoluted to analyze the N-containing group distribution (Fig. 5b–f).

The –NH– of PIP will form the tertiary amides after reacting with TMC. Hence, the TFC membrane surface contains two groups: the tertiary amide and the unreacted –NH– of PIP (Fig. 5b). After the membranes were modified with CQDs–NH2, all membranes showed the peak of –NH2 belonging to CQDs–NH2, and the –NH– peak area changed (Fig. 5c–f). Hence, we calculated the ratios of the three N-containing groups (Table 1 ). Along with the CQDs–NH2 amounts increased from 0.5 % to 1.5 %, the –NH2 proportion increased from 5.93 % to 9.58 %, whereas the tertiary amide amounts decreased from 83.1%–76.62%. The lower reaction degree proved that the addition of CQDs–NH2 decreased the reaction degree and induced the selective layer to form loose structures. The increased –NH– proportion (from 10.97% to 13.80%) may stem from the unreacted –NH– of PIP structures and part groups of CQDs–NH2. When the CQDs–NH2 amounts were increased from 1.5% to 2%, the –NH2 amounts decreased (from 9.58% to 7.24%) and the tertiary amide amounts increased (from 76.62% to 82.36%). It may be caused by the reaction between CQDs–NH2 and TMC. The decreased –NH– amounts (from 13.80% to 10.40%) may be rationalized by the loose structures formed by the CQDs–NH2 and TMC, leading to the leaching out of the unreacted PIP molecules during the solvent displacement with water.

The membrane surface group components distribution may affect the membrane surface charge property in water. Hence, the zeta potential was used to observe the membrane surface charge properties (Fig. 6 ). The traditional TFC membrane surface was negatively charged within a wide pH scan (from 3.0 to 10.0). After the CQDs–NH2 was introduced, the membrane tends to show more positively charged properties. The isoelectric point was increased with the positively charged CQDs–NH2 amounts increased from 0.5% to 1.5%. When the CQDs–NH2 amounts were increased to 2%, the membrane shows a negative surface, which may be because the large amounts of CQDs–NH2 inhibited the interfacial polymerization process and more TMC groups will hydrolysis into −COOH, leading to a negatively charged surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The nanofiltration performance and possible modification mechanisms of the CQDs–NH2 modified CTFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the chemical structure and surface property changes, four salts with different radius and charges were used to test the membrane separation performance (Fig. 7 ). Due to the loose polyamide structures of the modified CTFC membranes, all membranes exhibit lower rejections compared with that of the traditional TFC membrane. The MgCl2 rejections are higher than that of Na2SO4 and MgSO4, meaning that the modified CTFC membrane shows higher rejection to the positively charged ion (Mg2+) than the negatively charged ions (SO4 2− and Cl−). The experimental results are consistent with the positively charged surface shown in Fig. 6a. However, as to the negatively charged C2%TFC membrane, the membrane exhibited higher MgCl2 rejection compared with Na2SO4. It may be illustrated by the loose membrane structures. The low membrane rejection performance allows most ions to pass through the pores of the selective layer. The internal positively charged CQDs–NH2 may produce electrostatic repulsion force to Mg2+ and electrostatic adsorption force to SO4 2− when the charged ions pass through the membrane pores. Hence, the negatively charged C2%TFC membranes exhibit higher MgCl2 rejection than Na2SO4.

Considering the lowest salt rejection of the C2%TFC membrane, we used the C2%TFC membrane to further test the membrane separation performances to the organic molecules. The chemical structures of the dyes are listed in Table S1. The traditional TFC membranes showed low permeance (6.2 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) with high dyes selectivities (Fig. 8 ). The modified C2%TFC membrane show much higher permeance with diverse dye rejections. The membrane exhibits low rejection to the small dye molecule (RhB) and high rejection to the large dye molecule (AB), proving that the membrane structure became loose than the pristine TFC membrane. When the dye solution was used as the feed, the C2%TFC membrane permeance was decreased compared with the pure water. It may be rationalized by the decreased trans-membrane pressure and membrane pores blockage caused by the charged organic dyes and the C2%TFC membrane.

Based on all the changes in membrane morphology, internal structure, surface property, and separation performance, we analyzed the different membrane formation mechanisms, as shown in Fig. 9 . In the traditional IP process taking PIP and TMC as monomers, the PIP molecules stored in the PES substrate pores will diffuse to the interface and react with TMC in the n-hexane. The reaction will finish until the formation of the intact TFC selective layer formation and the PIP molecules below the selective layer cannot breakthrough the TFC network pores. Hence, the pristine TFC selective layer structure was dense to reject most of the small molecules and divalent salts (Fig. 9a–c). After the CQDs–NH2 was introduced, the reaction at the interface changed. First, impelled by the electrostatic attraction between the CQDs–NH2 (positive) and the PES substrate, part of the CQDs–NH2 particles will adsorb on the PES substrate surface and block the pores, which will inhibit the nether PIP diffusion to the interface to react with TMC. Second, the adsorbed CQDs–NH2 will occupy the space and prevent the formation of the dense polyamide network structure (Fig. 9e). Third, the –NH2 group of CQDs–NH2 may also react with TMC during the interfacial polymerization process, which will produce competition with the reaction between PIP and TMC and decrease the reaction degree (Fig. 9e & Table 1). Therefore, the CQDs–NH2 modified CTFC membranes exhibit more looser polyamide network structures, showing low rejection to the divalent salts and small dye molecules (Fig. 9f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Precise separation performance evaluation of the CTFC membrane

ce:section.ce:para:
In this work, we reported a CQDs–NH2 modified high-performance nanofiltration membrane with precise molecular separation property. The conclusion can be drawn as follows:
(1)
By incorporating the CQDs–NH2 into the aqueous solution, the modified membrane permeance was boosted significantly (from 6.2 to 38.44 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) compared with the pristine TFC membrane.
(2)
The intermolecular and electrostatic force between the CQDs–NH2, the charged PES polymeric membranes, and the charged reactive monomers may reformulate the interfacial polymerization reaction and regulate the membrane morphology, structural and surficial properties for improved separation performance.
(3)
By using the space occupied by the CQDs–NH2 particles and the large CTFC network pores formed by the CQDs–NH2 and TMC reaction, the prepared loose nanofiltration can separate two dyes precisely.

The study makes use of the competitive reaction between the reactive monomers to decrease the reaction degree and improve membrane performance, which may offer new sights in high-performance loose nanofiltration membrane preparation.
We honestly state that all of this manuscript or its contents have not been published previously and have no competing financial interest.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the research fundings provided by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFA0211002), Fok Ying Tung Education Foundation (171062), Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Fund Outstanding Youth Project (BK20190037), Tianjin Synthetic Biotechnology Innovation Capacity Improvement Project (TSBICIP-KJGG-003-20).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cherd.2021.04.031.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
